segments.s322-g41: This is a short clause with the verb after its object: 'سخنهای گودرز' = 'the words/speech of Goudarz' (possessive noun phrase) followed by the past tense verb 'بشنید' ('he heard') and the subject/name 'گیو' (Giv). The word order is object–verb–subject here, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s322-g42: This clause expresses action: the preposition 'ز' ('from') marks source, 'لشکر' is the noun 'army', then the compound action is formed by the verb-stem 'گزین' + auxiliary/past 'کرد' → 'selected/chose', and the direct object 'مردان نیو' ('new men') follows. In classical/poetic Persian the verb can split and follow its object(s).
segments.s322-g43: 'برآشفت' is a past form meaning 'became agitated/angry'; 'و' links clauses; 'اندیشه اندر گرفت' is a quasi‑idiomatic expression where 'اندیشه' ('thought') and the transitive verb 'گرفت' ('took/seized') combine to mean 'thought took hold' or 'fell to thinking'. Note the verb 'گرفت' is in past tense and the preposition 'اندر' ('in/within') marks the domain of the taking.
segments.s322-g44: Here 'ز' ('from') marks origin, 'ایران' names the land, 'ره' is 'way/road', and 'سیستان' is the destination; the verb 'برگرفت' (past) literally 'took up' — together meaning 'he took up the road from Iran toward Sistan.' Word order places the place-phrases before the verb 'برگرفت.'
segments.s322-g45: 'چو' here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; 'نزدیک' ('near') precedes the following nouns; 'دستان' in context appears as a name/term (not the literal 'hands' or 'tales'), then 'و رستم' ('and Rostam'); the past verb 'رسید' ('arrived/reached') closes the clause — standard SOV order in poetic Persian with the verb final.
segments.s322-g46: 'بگفت' is the past tense 'he said/told' introducing reported material; 'آن شگفتی' ('that marvel') is the object, and the relative clause 'که دید و شنید' ('that he saw and heard') uses two coordinated past verbs sharing the same subject. The conjunction 'که' introduces the content of what was seen/heard.
segments.s322-g47: 'غمی گشت' is an impersonal or experiential phrase meaning 'grief arose' or 'he became sorrowful' with 'گشت' as past of 'to become'; 'پس' links to what follows ('then/afterwards'); 'نامور زال' is the nominative phrase 'the famed Zal' (name + epithet) followed by 'گفت' ('said') which introduces his speech. Word order places the state-clause before the speech tag.
segments.s322-g48: The conjunction 'که' introduces reported content ('that'); 'گشتیم' is first‑person plural past 'we became' (speaker including self and another); the phrase 'با رنج بسیار' ('with much hardship') is an adjunct of manner, and 'جفت' ('pair; mate') is the predicate noun, so the clause literally states 'that we became a pair with great suffering.'
segments.s322-g9: همه داستانها is the subject phrase ('all the stories'); زدند is the 3rd-person plural past of زدن used poetically here to mean 'recounted' or 'sang/told' rather than 'struck'; از introduces the source/agent ('by/from') and مهان names those agents ('guests' or assembled visitors). Word order places the subject before the verb and the agent after از.
segments.s322-g10: بزرگان و فرزانگان جهان is a coordinated noun phrase in apposition to مهان: 'the great ones and the sages of the world.' جهان is a broadizing noun ('the world') specifying the scope of those great and wise people.
segments.s322-g11: پدر is the subject 'the father'; گیو را uses را to mark Giv as the addressee ('to Giv'); گفت is the past verb 'said'. کای is a vocative particle ('O') addressing the person, and نیکبخت is the vocative epithet 'fortunate one.' The clause therefore means 'the father said to Giv, “O fortunate one...”'.
segments.s322-g12: همیشه is an adverb 'always/ever' modifying the participle پرستنده, which is an active participle functioning as a predicate complement meaning 'worshipper/devotee.' تاج و تخت ('crown and throne') are the objects/fields of devotion. The verbless predication is typical in poetry: '(you are) ever a worshipper of crown and throne.'
segments.s322-g13: از ایران is a prepositional phrase indicating scope or cause ('for/from Iran'); بسی is an adverb 'much' modifying رنج ('suffering/hardship'); برداشتی is 2nd-person singular past of برداشتن used here to mean 'you endured/you took upon yourself.' Together: 'You endured much hardship for Iran.'
segments.s322-g14: بر و بوم و پیوند is a coordination of nouns ('lands and homes and bonds/alliances'); بگذاشتی is the 2nd-person singular past (with a prefixal b-) of گذاشتن meaning 'you placed/established' — here 'you set up/established (homes and alliances).' The three nouns list the things the subject has established.
segments.s322-g15: به پیش آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'came before/appeared' or 'arose'; اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'; یکی تیره کار is a noun phrase 'a certain dark/ominous affair' (تیره here 'ominous' or 'ill-omened') that has come to pass.
segments.s322-g16: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; آن را is the accusative 'that (thing)' marked by را; نشاید is an impersonal/formal negative meaning 'it is not fitting/it should not be'; the following که داریم خوار is a subordinate clause where داریم is used in sense of 'we (should) be' or 'we become' and خوار is the predicate adjective 'despised/humbled.' Together: 'which it is not fitting that we be/have (made) despised.'
segments.s322-g33: A noun phrase in apposition: ستاره‌شناسان (plural) = 'the stargazers/astrologers', followed by the place name کابلستان which identifies their region. In classical/poetic Persian the ezāfe linking may be omitted and juxtaposition is used to show possession/origin.
segments.s322-g34: Another nominal phrase: همه ('all') precedes پاکرویان (plural noun 'the pious/virtuous') and زابلستان names their region. Word order is determiner/quantifier + noun + place; the phrase means 'all the virtuous of Zabulistan.'
segments.s322-g35: بیارید is an imperative/jussive verb form meaning 'bring' or 'let (someone) bring' (addressing a group or issuing a command). ز این در is a prepositional phrase 'from this door/gate' (ز = 'from', در = 'door/gate'), and یکی انجمن = 'one assembly' (یکی = 'one', انجمن = 'assembly/meeting') is the object being brought.
segments.s322-g36: به ایران is a directional phrase 'into/ toward Iran' (به = 'towards/into', ایران = place). خرامید is a jussive/past-stem form meaning 'stride/prance' used here as a hortative 'let them stride' or describing the action; با خویشتن = 'with themselves' indicates accompaniment or that they go together. Word order: direction + verb + accompaniment.
segments.s322-g37: شد (past/auxiliary) + subject: classical Persian often places the verb before the subject, so شد این پادشاهی = 'this kingship became'. پر از means 'full of' (پر = 'full (of)', followed by از + noun), گفتگوی = 'conversation/talk' is the complement introduced by از.
segments.s322-g38: چو + past verb (پوشید) = 'when/after he concealed' (چو = 'when'); پوشید here means 'to cover/conceal'. خسرو is the agent (the king—proper name), ز ما = 'from us' (ز = 'from'), رای و روی = 'his counsel (rāy) and face' where رای = 'opinion/counsel' and روی = 'face'; the structure indicates Khosrow hid both his counsel and his face from the speakers.
segments.s322-g39: فگندیم is a past first‑person plural verb 'we cast away/discarded' (transitive). هرگونه رایی = 'every/any kind of counsel/opinion' (هرگونه = 'any kind', رایی = 'opinion/counsel') functions as the direct object, and ز بن = 'from the root/foundation' is a prepositional phrase indicating source or basis.
segments.s322-g40: This clause has a verb-subject order typical of classical Persian: ز دستان = 'from tales/stories' (ز = 'from', دستان here = 'tales/stories') is an adverbial/source phrase; گشاید (third‑person singular) = 'opens/unfolds' (گشاید with the sense 'opens/sets forth'), همی is an adverbial particle marking ongoing or emphatic action, and این سخن = 'this speech/word' is the subject placed after the verb.
segments.s322-g1: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction) introduces a circumstantial clause. یک هفته بگذشت is a past-tense time phrase ('one week passed'); بگذشت is past of گذشتن meaning 'to pass (time)'. ننمود روی is a negated past form (ن + نمود) meaning 'did not show (his) face' — روی here = 'face, countenance'. The clause sets the time/condition for what follows.
segments.s322-g2: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/appeared'. یکی here means 'a certain / one' (introducing a single instance of something). غلغل و گفتگوی are two nouns joined by و: an emphatic pair 'a clamor and conversation' — together they name the kind of disturbance that arose.
segments.s322-g3: همه پهلوانان is the subject 'all the champions'. شدند is past of شدن 'they became' / 'they assembled' here used as a copula/verb of becoming; انجمن is the complement 'assembly/council'. The word order is subject + verb + predicate as in classical Persian.
segments.s322-g4: This is a noun phrase describing the assembly: بزرگان فرزانه = 'great, wise ones' (adjective + noun), و links the next phrase; رای زن is a compound meaning 'those who give counsel / counsellors' (رای = counsel/plan, زن here forms an agentive/participial notion in classical usage).
segments.s322-g5: چو... و چون is a repeated simile/temporal marker here meaning 'as' or 'when' — introducing the speakers. گودرز and طوس are proper names; نوذرنژاد is an epithet attached to طوس meaning 'of the lineage of Nowzar' (a patronymic).
segments.s322-g6: سخن رفت is an impersonal/idiomatic expression 'speech went' i.e. 'they spoke' or 'discussion took place' (رفت = past of رفتن 'to go' used idiomatically). چندی is an adverb 'for a while'; ز introduces the topic 'about' or 'concerning' (poetic use of ز = از); بیداد و داد are the paired topics 'injustice and justice'.
segments.s322-g7: ز at the start is the poetic preposition 'about/concerning'. کردار شاهان is a genitive 'the deeds of kings'; برتر منش is an adjectival phrase 'superior / noble character' (برتر modifying منش). The phrase lists one topic of discussion.
segments.s322-g8: ز again introduces another topic 'about'. یزدان پرستان is 'the worshippers/devotees of God' (یزدان = God, پرستان = worshippers). وز here is actually a fused form meaning 'and from' (و + ز) common in poetry; it links the next item 'بدکنش' = 'the ill‑doer / wrongdoer'. (The lexical list given does not include this fused conjunction sense, so it's annotated separately.)
segments.s322-g49: به رستم (prepositional phrase) marks the indirect object 'to Rostam'. چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus' with گفت (past 3rd‑singular) reporting speech; کز introduces a subordinate clause 'that/...'. بخردان is a plural, poetic form meaning 'the wise' and functions as the head of the following noun phrase.
segments.s322-g50: ستاره‌شناسان و هم موبدان is a coordinated noun phrase: ستاره‌شناسان 'the astrologers' coordinated with موبدان 'the mobeds/priests' by و. هم adds inclusive emphasis 'also/even' to موبدان; word order is straightforward enumeration.
segments.s322-g51: ز زابل (poetic preposition) = 'from Zabul'; بخوان and بخواه are imperatives (2nd‑person commands) meaning 'call/summon' and 'ask/summon' respectively; they are coordinated by و. The short poetic ز before place names marks source.
segments.s322-g52: بدان is an imperative/hortative form (b + دان) meaning roughly 'let it be known / inform (him/them)'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / in order that'; بیایند is a present‑subjunctive (3rd‑pl) expressing desired outcome 'they may come'. با ما به راه is a prepositional phrase 'with us on the road'.
segments.s322-g53: شدند is past 3rd‑plural ('they became/assembled'); in classical/poetic Persian this verb often announces that a meeting took place. انجمن is the noun 'assembly' and موبدان و ردان are coordinated as participants in that assembly. Word order (verb before noun) is common in poetry.
segments.s322-g54: ستاره‌شناسان و هم بخردان again enumerates participants: ستاره‌شناسان 'the astrologers' coordinated with بخردان 'the wise', and هم gives the sense 'also/too'. This completes the list of who composed the gathering.
segments.s322-g55: همه 'everyone' is the subject; سوی marks direction 'toward'. دستان is the target of that direction (here a place/name or 'Dastān'); نهادند (past 3rd‑pl) + روی forms the compound action 'to set/turn (their) face' — poetic inversion places روی after the verb. The line uses the idiom 'روی نهادن (به)' = 'to turn toward'.
segments.s322-g56: ز زابر به ایران is a source→destination phrase 'from Zabul to Iran' (ز = from, به = to). نهادند روی repeats the idiom 'they set/turned their faces' with روی as 'face' and the verb in past plural; together the phrase means 'they turned their faces toward Iran.'
segments.s322-g17: بباید here is a modal 'must/it is necessary' (classical form), followed by شدن as an infinitival/auxiliary 'to be/go', so the phrase means 'it must be that one goes' or 'one must go toward...'. سوی is the preposition 'toward', and زابلستان is the place name 'Zabulistan'. Word order is modal + verb + direction (typical classical Persian).
segments.s322-g18: سواری is a noun 'rider/horseman'. فرستی is the 2nd-person singular subjunctive/imperfective form of فرستادن used here as an instruction ('send' / 'you should send'). به is the preposition 'to/for', and کابلستان is the place 'Kabulistan'. The clause is an imperative/optative instruction following the previous necessity.
segments.s322-g19: This is a sequence of prepositional phrases and a verb of saying. به زابل = 'to Zabul', به رستم = 'to Rostam'. بگویی is the classical subjunctive/imperative form of گفتن used as 'tell' or 'may you tell' (second person), and که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. Word order places destination(s) before the reporting verb.
segments.s322-g20: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' / 'by' (indicating cause or agency). یزدان = 'God'. بپیچید is past 3rd-person formed with the classical prefix بـ + پیچید, meaning 'he/it turned aside' (the ب- is the past marker common in older Persian poetry). و = 'and'. گم is 'lost' used as predicate adjective, کرد is the past of 'to do/make' serving to mean 'made lost' or simply 'lost' in combination, and راه = 'way/path'.
segments.s322-g21: در بار is a fixed classical phrase 'in the court/at the royal assembly' (در = in; بار used for the royal presence/occasion). بر نامداران = 'against/vis-à-vis the notable ones' (بر + noun), and ببست is the 3rd-person past of بستن ('he shut/closed'); the doubled b- is an archaic/poetic form of the past stem. Overall: 'he closed (the court) to the notables.'
segments.s322-g22: همانا is an emphatic 'verily/indeed'. که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/for'. با دیو = 'with a demon'. دارد is the present third‑person of داشتن 'has/possesses' used here as 'is holding/has' and نشست is a noun 'a sitting/meeting' (so دارد نشست = 'he has a meeting' / 'he is sitting with'). The phrase explains the reason for the court's closure.
segments.s322-g23: بسی here means 'much/many' (classical use). پوزش = 'apology/excuse' and خواهش = 'request/entreaty'; the coordinating و = 'and'. آراستیم is first‑person plural past of آراستن 'we arranged/presented'—in this context 'we offered/made many apologies and requests.'
segments.s322-g24: همی is an adverbial particle (here roughly 'thus/so/indeed'). زان = 'from that/therefore' pointing back to what was done. سخن = 'speech/words', کام او = 'his desire/wish' (کام = desire; او = he), and خواستیم is first‑person plural past of خواستن 'we desired/sought' (here 'we sought to fulfil his wish' or 'we sought his satisfaction'). Word order places the means and object before the verb, as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s322-g81: وگر begins a conditional ('if'); چاره این is the noun phrase 'this remedy/solution' with این pointing to the foregoing; برآید is a present/subjunctive-like verbal form meaning 'can be brought about / become possible' and governs the condition; به + گنج ('with/by treasure') indicates the means by which the remedy might be obtained.
segments.s322-g82: نبیند is a negative-stem present (literally 'will not see' / 'does not see') used here in the sense 'will not experience'; ز introduces the source/reason 'from/because of'; گنج درم lists two types of wealth ('treasure' then 'money/coin'); نیز 'also' links the two items; رنج is the noun 'suffering/hardship.' The phrase as a whole says that no hardship will be seen/experienced from treasure or money.
segments.s322-g83: همه پاسبانان گنج is a nominal phrase 'all the guardians of the treasure'; توایم is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning 'I am yours' or 'I am for you' and here predicated of the speaker: 'I am (for) all the treasure's guards.' Word order places the predicative توایم after the noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s322-g84: پر از درد 'full of pain' uses پر + از + noun to mean 'filled with X'; گریان is a participial adjective 'weeping' describing the speaker's state; ز + رنج 'because of (your) suffering' gives the cause; the final توایم repeats/underlines the speaker's stance 'I am (yours),' tying the lament to the addressee.
segments.s322-g85: چنین داد پاسخ places the adverbial چنین ('thus/so') before داد (past 'gave') and پاسخ ('answer'), so literally 'thus gave answer'; جهاندار is the agent 'the sovereign' and باز ('again') modifies the action — 'answered again.' The verb is simple past narrative.
segments.s322-g86: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'; از پهلوانان is a prepositional phrase 'of/from the champions'; نیم here functions as a qualifier meaning 'partly/ not wholly' and qualifies بی‌نیاز ('free from need' / 'independent'), so the clause means 'that (he) is not wholly independent of the champions' — i.e. he still depends on them to some extent.
segments.s322-g87: ولیکن is an adversative conjunction 'but/yet'; ندارم is present first-person 'I do not have' (negated possession/state); همی is a continuous/emphatic adverb (here 'still/ever'); دل به رنج ('heart toward suffering') is an idiomatic object phrase meaning 'a heart free from suffering' or, in this negative context, 'my heart is not free from suffering.' The clause states the speaker's continuing inner trouble.
segments.s322-g88: This is a coordinate list introduced by repeated ز 'from/because of' indicating causes: ز نیروی دست = 'from the strength/power of the hand' (i.e. military force), ز مردان = 'from men/warriors', و گنج = 'and treasure.' The phrase explains why the speaker's heart is not free from pain: because of forces, men, and wealth.
segments.s322-g89: نه negates the whole clause. در کشوری is a locative phrase ('in a country/realm') placed before the subject in poetic word order. دشمن is the (indefinite) subject and آمد پدید is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'came forth / appeared' (آمد + پدید = 'appeared').
segments.s322-g90: که introduces a relative clause ('that / which'). تیمار آن = 'that (thing)／its care' (آن refers back to the enemy). بد here functions adverbially/qualifyingly ('badly, arduous, severe'). بباید is the modal 'must/ought to' and کشید is the past form of کشیدن used here in the sense 'to be (treated / handled / endured)' (poetic/archaic usage). The clause means 'that would require (much) care/trouble to be dealt with.'
segments.s322-g91: یکی آرزو is 'a certain/a single desire' (subject). خواست is the past verb 'wished/wanted' or 'caused' in the sense 'made [it so]'. روشن precedes the possessed noun in poetic order and qualifies دلم ('my heart' — دلم is the substantive + -م possessive suffix). The whole line says that a single desire illuminated/cleared the speaker's heart.
segments.s322-g92: همی is a durative adverb ('still / continually'). دل is the topic/subject; از آن آرزو is a prepositional phrase 'from that desire'. نگسلم is a negative-subjunctive/negative-present form (ن + گسلم) meaning 'I will not be separated / I will not break away' — here 'not part from' or 'not be disunited from'.
segments.s322-g93: بدان is the fused preposition phrase به + آن (= 'by/through/with that' or 'from that'); آرزو is the noun 'desire.' دارم is the first‑person present 'I have' and اکنون 'now' sets the temporal frame; امید is the object 'hope.' Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect: 'By that desire I have now hope.'
segments.s322-g94: شب تیره ... روز سپید is a temporal phrase: شب تیره = 'the dark night', تا = 'until', گاه = 'the time/hour', روز سپید = 'the bright day.' The phrase means 'from the dark night until the hour of the bright day.' Note the adjective سپید follows روز in the usual poetic order.
segments.s322-g95: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' یابم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I find / should I find' used to mark the time clause. بگویم is the corresponding first‑person present/subjunctive/future idea 'I will say / I shall tell.' همه راز خویش = 'all (the) my secret(s)' where خویش is reflexive/possessive ('my/one's own') — the object of بگویم. The clause reads 'When I find [it], I will tell all my secrets.'
segments.s322-g96: برآرم is first‑person (subjunctive/intentive) 'I will lift up / I will utter.' نهان کرده is a perfect participial phrase ('having hidden' or 'which I have hidden') modifying آواز ('voice'). خویش is the reflexive possessive ('my/one's own'), here marking the voice as the speaker's. The sequence means 'I will lift up/utter the voice (which) I had hidden.'
segments.s322-g57: جهاندار is the subject meaning 'the world‑ruler/sovereign'. بر پای بد is a prepositional phrase literal 'on a bad foot' used idiomatically here to describe being ill or incapacitated; هفت روز is a duration placed after the circumstance and means 'for seven days'. Word order is subject + circumstantial phrase + duration, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s322-g58: به هشتم 'on the eighth (day)' gives the time. چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بفروخت is a past tense verb 'sold/parted with' placed before the noun in inverted (poetic) order; گیتی‌فروز is the noun (here a name or epithet) that functions as the subject of بفروخت. The clause therefore reads 'when Giti‑froz sold/parted with (it/him)'.
segments.s322-g59: ز در پرده is a locative/ablative phrase 'from (out of) the curtain/door' (ز = 'from'). برداشت is the past tense verb 'lifted/took up' and سالار بار is the subject + object (سالار 'the commander' and بار 'the burden/load') in the order of poetic diction: 'the commander lifted the load.'
segments.s322-g60: نشست is the past tense 'sat down'. از بر تخت زر شهریار is a prepositional phrase marking position/adjacency: از بر (a classical compound meaning 'by/at the side of') + تخت زر شهریار 'the king's golden throne' (تخت 'throne', زر 'gold', شهریار 'king'). Word order places the verb before the locative phrase, as common in poetry.
segments.s322-g61: همه پهلوانان is the nominal subject 'all the champions'. ابا موبدان here is best read as a genitive/qualifying phrase 'the priests' reluctance' or a note of reluctance associated with the mōbeds (موبدان); in context it marks the manner or attitude (they came despite or with a note of priests' reluctance). This is a compact, somewhat elliptical poetic expression rather than a full clause.
segments.s322-g62: برفتند is the past tense plural 'they went/advanced'. نزدیک is an adverb/preposition 'near' or 'close to', and شاه جهان is a title 'the king (ruler) of the world' (شاه 'king', جهان 'world'). The phrase means 'they advanced near the king of the world.'
segments.s322-g63: فراوان ببودند is a past construction meaning 'they were many' (فراوان 'many/abundant', ببودند classical past 'they were'). پیشش 'before him/in his presence' gives the locus. به پای is a phrase 'at the feet of' (به + پای) introducing the following noun phrase that indicates to whom they were attending.
segments.s322-g64: بزرگان is the plural 'the great ones/elders'. با دانش و رهنمای is a coordinating phrase 'with knowledge and (as) guide(s)' where با introduces qualities/companions: دانش 'learning/knowledge' and رهنمای 'guide/adviser'; together they qualify the بزرگان as learned counsellors.
segments.s322-g97: شما is the 2nd-person plural/formal pronoun placed before the verb for emphasis. بازگردید is a 2nd-person-plural jussive/imperative form meaning 'return' (used here as a wish/command). پیروز و شاد are predicate adjectives (‘victorious and glad’) placed after the verb to describe the state in which the addressee should return.
segments.s322-g98: بد اندیشه is a noun phrase 'evil/bad thought'; بر دل is a prepositional phrase 'upon the heart' (بر used spatially/figuratively). مدارید here is a 2nd-person-plural jussive/imperative-like form governing یاد, giving a prohibitive/advisory sense 'do not harbor/let ... remain' (classical usage where the verb governs the following noun). یاد is the noun 'remembrance/memory' functioning as the object/thing not to be entertained.
segments.s322-g99: همه is a universal quantifier 'all/every', placed before the noun. پهلوانان is the plural noun 'champions/heroes', and آزادمرد is an epithet/adjective following it ('noble man' or 'man of freedom') in apposition to characterize those heroes.
segments.s322-g100: بر او is a prepositional phrase 'to/at him' (بر with the sense 'toward/to'). خواندند is past tense 3rd-person-plural 'they sang/they cried out' (perfective). آفرینی is an exclamatory vocable 'Afarin!/Bravo!' and به درد ('for/at his sorrow') uses به + noun to mark the occasion/reason (they uttered the shout with respect to his grief).
segments.s322-g101: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'; ایشان is the 3rd-person plural pronoun 'they' and is the subject of برفتند, the past-tense verb 'they went/they departed'. پیروز شاه is a noun phrase 'the victorious king' (adjective + noun) identifying the speaker of the main clause that follows.
segments.s322-g102: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past-tense verb 'he commanded/said'. تا functions here as a complementizer 'that/so that', introducing what he ordered. پرده بارگاه is the direct object phrase 'the curtain of the palace' (پرده + بارگاه), with پرده (curtain) modified by بارگاه (palace) in genitival relation.
segments.s322-g103: فروهشت here denotes something being spread/let down (passive/resultative sense 'was spread/let down'). و links actions. بنشست is 3rd-person-singular past 'he sat down'. گریان is a predicative/participial adjective 'weeping', and به درد is a prepositional phrase 'with/in grief' that qualifies his sitting and weeping.
segments.s322-g104: همی بود is an imperfective past construction 'he was still/kept on', with همی giving continued action. پیچان is a participial/adjectival form 'twisting/convulsing' describing the face, and رخ لاژورد is a noun + modifier 'face lapis-lazuli' (lažvard as a color adjective), so the clause says his face kept twitching and was lapis-blue.
segments.s322-g73: همه بودنیها is the noun phrase 'all beings/that which exists' serving as the thing to be known; the prepositional phrase به روشن‌روان ('by/with bright‑souled') gives an instrumental/qualifying sense. The verb is in the next segment, so this segment front‑positions the object and the means before the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s322-g74: بدانی is second‑person singular subjunctive (used here as an exhortation: 'know' / 'you should know'); it takes the preceding noun phrase as its object. The second به introduces another instrumental phrase (به کردار و دانش جوان), where کردار = 'deed/action' and دانش جوان = 'youthful learning' (دانش modified by جوان after it).
segments.s322-g75: بندگانیم is a present‑tense copular verb form meaning 'we are servants/subjects' (the -یم ending encodes 'we'); همه quantifies it. در پیش شاه = 'in the presence of / before the king', where در پیش functions as a prepositional phrase locating the state.
segments.s322-g76: چه کردیم is a rhetorical question in past tense ('what have we done' / 'what did we do'). In بر ما چرا بست راه the preposition بر marks the affected party ('against/on us'), چرا = 'why' asks for reason, بست is the third‑person past 'closed/blocked', and راه = 'road/way' is the object (i.e. 'why has he closed the way against us?').
segments.s322-g77: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). غم ز دریاست = 'sorrow is/from the sea' (ز = 'from/of'). خشکی کنیم uses the first‑person plural subjunctive/optative ('let us make [it] dry' / 'we will make dry'), a volitional form expressing resolution in poetry.
segments.s322-g78: همه quantifies the following object. چادر خاک مشکی is a compound description ('a tent/cloak of black dust') where خاک مشکی = 'black dust' (خاک noun + مشکی adjective); کنیم again is the volitional 'we will/may make', so the whole line asserts we will turn all tents into black‑dust cloaks (i.e. cover/blacken them).
segments.s322-g79: وگر is another conditional 'and if / but if'. کوه باشد is a subjunctive clause 'if there be a mountain' (باشد here marking the hypothetical/existential). ز بن ('from the root/base') indicates the point of removal, and برکنیم is first‑person plural subjunctive/optative meaning 'let us remove/uproot' (a determined volitional act).
segments.s322-g80: به marks instrumentality ('with/by'); خنجر دل دشمنان is a genitive chain meaning 'the dagger [to/into] the hearts of the enemies' (دل دشمنان = 'the hearts of the enemies'). بشکنیم is first‑person plural subjunctive/optative here used as 'we will break' (i.e. we will break their hearts).
segments.s322-g65: چون here is the subordinating conjunction 'as/when' (archaic/classical usage) introducing a temporal clause. دید is a simple past 'saw/observed'. بنواختشان is a past verb with the third-person singular prefixed stem (بنواخت) plus the object pronoun -شان 'them'; in this poetic context it functions as a transitive past verb meaning 'struck/played for' or 'honoured/received' them (poetic verb use). Word order is typical for elevated/poetic Persian: Subject (جهاندار) + temporal conjunction + past verb phrase.
segments.s322-g66: به رسم کیان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'according to the manner/custom of the Kayan (kings)'; به takes the sense 'by/according to'. پایگه ساختشان is a verbal clause: ساخت (past 'made/established') with the object pronoun -شان 'for them', so 'he established/made for them a پایگه (a station/seat/foundation)'. The phrase as a whole links as the result or manner of how he treated them.
segments.s322-g67: این گروه‌سازی 'از آن نامداران خسروپرست' is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of those famed, king‑worshipping ones' (از here = 'from/of those'). نامداران is a plural attributive noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones' and خسروپرست is a compound adjective/noun 'devotees of the king/royal‑worshippers' modifying them.
segments.s322-g68: کس here is used as 'no one' (negated by the verb); the negation is carried by the verbal prefix ن- in ننشست (past tense: 'did not sit/down' or 'did not yield'). از پای ننشست is an idiomatic phrase 'did not sit from the foot' = 'did not give way/step down'; و نگشاد دست is coordinated by و 'and', with نگشاد being the negated past participle/verb 'did not open' (ن- + گشاد) and دست the direct object 'hand' (i.e. 'did not open/extend the hand' — did not reach out or accept). The clause thus yields a double negative action: none of them yielded nor reached out.
segments.s322-g69: گشادند is a third‑person plural past (or stative) form 'were opened/they opened' used here with لب 'lips' as its grammatical object/subject in inverted poetic order (literally 'opened were the lips'). کی functions as a temporal 'when' ('when'), so کی سپهر روان together qualifies the clause ('when the moving/flowing heaven'). سپهر روان is a two‑word description 'the sky/heaven (سپهر) that is روان (flowing, moving or alive)'; روان is an adjectival/epithet.
segments.s322-g70: جهاندار is the appositive/continuation naming the actor 'the sovereign'. با is the preposition 'with' linking the sovereign to his qualities; داد and روشن‌روان are conjoined attributes ('justice' and 'bright/clear‑souled' respectively). Grammatically this phrase attaches to the preceding clause as the agent described by these virtues.
segments.s322-g71: توانایی و فر شاهی is a coordinated noun phrase 'ability (power) and the royal glory (فر شاهی)'. تراست is an archaic/poetic fused predicate: ترا + است = 'it is to you' or 'belongs to you' (third‑person copula with the dative/possessive clitic). So the whole clause states possession/attribution: 'Power and royal glory are yours.'
segments.s322-g72: ز ... تا ... is a poetic 'from ... to ...' range construction: ز خورشید 'from the sun' تا پشت ماهی 'to behind the moon'. تراست repeats the same archaic copular/possessive 'it is yours' for emphasis. Note that پشت here means 'behind/rear' and the prepositional z (ز) is the older/poetic form of از.
segments.s322-g25: فراوان (an adverb) modifies شنید; شنید is the simple past 'he heard'. ایچ here intensifies the negation («not at all») before پاسخ نداد, where نداد is the past negative of دادن 'to give' used idiomatically for 'to give an answer' (i.e. 'to reply'). Word order is emphatic: adverb – verb – negative particle – object – verb.
segments.s322-g26: دلش is the noun + enclitic possessive ('his heart'). خیره is an adjectival/predicative participle meaning 'dumbstruck/astonished'. بینیم is the 1st‑person plural form of دیدن used here in a narrative sense ('we see' / 'we perceived' — a classical/poetic present used like a past/historical vision). و links the next clause; سر پر ز باد = 'a head full of wind' (پر ... ز = 'full of/of/from'), a poetic metaphor meaning empty‑headed or puffed up.
segments.s322-g27: بترسیم is the 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive 'we fear' (classical narrative hortative/subjunctive). کو here functions as the subordinating particle 'that/if' (an archaic usage of کو = که). هیچو is an emphatic negative/conditional particle (classical/poetic 'ever/at all' or 'by any chance' in conditional clauses). کاووس شاه is a noun + title in apposition ('Kaavus, the king').
segments.s322-g28: شود is the present/subjunctive/auxiliary 'may become' (classical usage expressing potential). کژ is a predicate adjective 'crooked/wrong, perverse'. و coordinates the next idea. دیوش (دیو + ش in the text form) refers to 'a demon' (agent). بپیچد is the present/subjunctive of پیچیدن 'to turn/lead astray' (b‑ prefix marks the verb); ز راه = 'from the way/path' (ز = poetic 'from'). The clause expresses fear of a possible change of conduct and demonic leading astray.
segments.s322-g29: شما پهلوانید is the copular statement 'you are champions' (شما + adjectival/noun predicate + ‑ید ending for 2nd‑person plural/polite). و links the second predicate; داناترید is another predicate with the same personal ending meaning 'you are learned' (here a quality attributed to 'you').
segments.s322-g30: به هر بودنی = 'in/with every being/any kind of existence' (به + هر + بودنی, where بودنی is a substantive participle 'that which is/being'). بر تواناترید: بر functions as 'over/against' marking the domain in which the comparison holds; تواناترید is a comparative/attributive form meaning 'you are more powerful / superior in strength' (again 2nd‑person plural ending).
segments.s322-g31: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. هرکه او هست is the relative clause 'whoever he is' (هرکه + pronoun + copula هست). پاکیزه‌رای is a compound adjective 'of pure taste/pure‑minded' predicated of that person; the compound is a poetic attribute describing disposition or taste.
segments.s322-g32: This is a poetic list introduced by ز (z = 'from/of'): ز قنوج و ز دنور 'from Kannauj and from Denur' (the manuscript writes وز as the sequence و + ز). The following و connects items: مرغ و مای = 'birds and wine' (items/tributes or possessions). The sequence enumerates origins/items associated with the person just described.

sentences.s322-s21: Giv heard Goudarz's words, and from the army selected new men.
sentences.s322-s22: He grew agitated and fell to thinking; he took the road from Iran toward Sistan.
sentences.s322-s23: When he came near Dastan and Rostam, he told the marvel that he had seen and heard.
sentences.s322-s24: Sorely grieved, the famed Zal said that we became paired after much suffering.
sentences.s322-s5: All the tales were recounted by the guests — the great and the wise of the world.
sentences.s322-s6: Giv's father said to him, 'O fortunate one, ever a worshipper of crown and throne.'
sentences.s322-s7: You have borne much hardship for Iran, and you established homelands and alliances.
sentences.s322-s8: Now an ominous affair has arisen before us — one which it is not fitting that we become despised over.
sentences.s322-s17: The stargazers of Kabulistan and all the virtuous of Zabulistan.
sentences.s322-s18: Bring an assembly from this gate; let them stride into Iran with themselves.
sentences.s322-s19: This kingdom became full of talk when Khosrow concealed his counsel and face from us.
sentences.s322-s20: We cast away every kind of counsel from the root; from tales this speech still unfolds.
sentences.s322-s1: When a week had passed and no face was shown, an uproar and conversation arose.
sentences.s322-s2: All the champions assembled — a council of great sages and counsellors.
sentences.s322-s3: As Goudarz and as Tūs, descendant of Nowzar, spoke, the talk went on for a while about injustice and justice.
sentences.s322-s4: They spoke (also) of the deeds of kings and of noble character, of the worshippers of God and of the evil‑doer.
sentences.s322-s25: He said to Rostam thus: that among the wise were the astrologers and also the mobeds.
sentences.s322-s26: Summon (them) from Zabul and request (them) from Kabul; let it be known so that they come with us on the road.
sentences.s322-s27: A gathering was formed — the mobeds and the rdan, the astrologers and the wise as well.
sentences.s322-s28: They all turned their faces toward Dastān; from Zabul they set their faces toward Iran.
sentences.s322-s9: It is necessary to go toward Zabulistan; send a rider to Kabulistan.
sentences.s322-s10: Go to Zabul and tell Rostam that the king, by God's will, turned aside and lost the way.
sentences.s322-s11: He shut the court against the notables, for indeed he is sitting with a demon.
sentences.s322-s12: We made many apologies and entreaties; thus we sought to satisfy his desire by that speech.
sentences.s322-s41: And if this remedy can be attained with treasure, he will not experience hardship because of treasure or money.
sentences.s322-s42: I am, for you, every guardian of the treasure; filled with pain, I weep because of your suffering.
sentences.s322-s43: Thus the sovereign answered again, that he is not wholly independent of the champions.
sentences.s322-s44: But I do not have my heart free from suffering, because of the power of arms, of men, and of treasure.
sentences.s322-s45: No enemy appeared in any land that would require such trouble to deal with.
sentences.s322-s46: A certain desire made my heart bright; still my heart will not part from that desire.
sentences.s322-s47: From that desire I now have hope, from the dark night until the hour of the bright day.
sentences.s322-s48: When I find it, I will tell all my secret and raise the voice I have kept hidden.
sentences.s322-s29: The sovereign lay ill for seven days; on the eighth, when Giti‑froz sold (or gave up), ...
sentences.s322-s30: From behind the curtain the commander lifted the burden and sat down beside the king's golden throne.
sentences.s322-s31: All the champions, (despite) the priests' reluctance, advanced close to the sovereign of the world.
sentences.s322-s32: There were many before him, at the feet of the elders, learned and guiding.
sentences.s322-s49: May you return victorious and joyful; do not harbor any evil thought in your heart's memory.
sentences.s322-s50: All the champions and noble men cried out 'Afarin' to him for his sorrow.
sentences.s322-s51: When they departed, the victorious king commanded (that) the palace curtain...
sentences.s322-s52: He had it let down and sat down weeping in grief; his face kept twisting and was lapis-blue.
sentences.s322-s37: Know all beings by their bright souls, by their deeds and youthful learning.
sentences.s322-s38: We are all servants before the king; what have we done, and why has he closed the way against us?
sentences.s322-s39: If sorrow is from the sea, let us make it dry; let us make every tent a cloak of black dust.
sentences.s322-s40: And if there be a mountain, we will uproot it from its base; with the dagger we will break the hearts of enemies.
sentences.s322-s33: When the sovereign saw them, he greeted/honored them; in the custom of the Kayan kings he set up a station for them.
sentences.s322-s34: Of those renowned, king‑worshipping men, no one sat down (i.e. yielded) nor opened his hand.
sentences.s322-s35: The heavens' lips were opened (i.e. the sky's mouth opened) — the sovereign, with justice and a radiant soul.
sentences.s322-s36: Power and the royal glory belong to you; from the sun to behind the moon it is yours.
sentences.s322-s13: He heard much but gave no answer; we saw his heart stunned and his head full of wind.
sentences.s322-s14: We fear that, if Kaavus becomes king, he may turn crooked and a demon may lead him astray from the way.
sentences.s322-s15: You are champions and learned; in every kind of being you are the more powerful.
sentences.s322-s16: Now whoever he may be — of pure taste from Kannauj and from Denur, with birds and wine.
